content: The area of the region bounded by the parabola $y^2=27 x$ and the line $x=1$ is ________ sq.units.
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Required area }\\ &\begin{aligned} & =2 \int_0^1 y d x \\ & =2 \int_0^1 \sqrt{27 x} d x \\ & =6 \sqrt{3} \int_0^1 x^{\frac{1}{2}} d x \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =6 \sqrt{3}\left[\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right]_0^1 \\ & =4 \sqrt{3}\left(1^{\frac{3}{2}}-0\right) \\ & =4 \sqrt{3} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: If the area bounded by the curve $x^2=4 y, \mathrm{X}$-axis and the line $x=4$ is divided into equal areas by the line $x=\alpha$, then the value of $\alpha$ is …
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { According to the given condition, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & A_1=A_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \int_0^\alpha \frac{x^2}{4} d x=\int_\alpha^4 \frac{x^2}{4} d x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^\alpha=\frac{1}{4}\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_\alpha^4 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{12}\left(\alpha^3-0\right)=\frac{1}{12}\left(4^3-\alpha^3\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha^3=64-\alpha^3 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \alpha^3=64 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha^3=32 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=(32)^{\frac{1}{3}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The area of smaller part between the circle $x^2+y^2=4$ and the line $x=1$ is _________ sq.units.
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Area of smaller part }=2 \int_1^2 \sqrt{4-x^2} d x \\ & =2\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{4-x^2}+2 \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}\right]_1^2 \\ & =2\left[2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}-\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right] \\ & =\frac{4 \pi}{3}-\sqrt{3} \end{aligned} $$

content: The area bounded by the curve $y=4 x-x^2$ and X - axis in square units, is $\qquad$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_0^4\left(4 x-x^2\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\frac{4 x^2}{2}-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^4=32-\frac{64}{3} \\ & =\frac{32}{3} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: The area bounded by the parabolas $y=9 x^2, y=\frac{x^2}{16}$ and the line $y=1$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =2 \int_0^1\left(4 \sqrt{y}-\frac{\sqrt{y}}{3}\right) \mathrm{d} y \\ & =2\left[4\left(\frac{y^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right)-\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{y^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right)\right]_0^1 \\ & =2\left(\frac{8}{3}-\frac{2}{9}\right) \\ & =2\left(\frac{22}{9}\right) \\ & =\frac{44}{9} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: The area bounded by the curve $y=x^2+3, y=x, x=3$ and $y$-axis is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_0^3\left(x^2+3-x\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\frac{x^3}{3}+3 x-\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^3 \\ & =9+9-\frac{9}{2} \\ & =\frac{27}{2} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: The area bounded by the curve $x^2=8 y$ and the straight line $x-8 y+2=0$ is
explanation:
The points of intersection of $x^2=8 y$ and $x-8 y+2=0$ are $\left(-1, \frac{1}{8}\right)$ and $\left(2, \frac{1}{2}\right)$.

$$ \begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_{-1}^2\left(\frac{2+x}{8}\right) \mathrm{d} x-\int_{-1}^2 \frac{x^2}{8} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\frac{1}{8}\left[2 x+\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{-1}^2-\frac{1}{8}\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-1}^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{8}\left(\frac{15}{2}\right)-\frac{1}{8}(3) \\ & =\frac{9}{16} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: If a curve $y=a \sqrt{x}+b x$ passes through the point $(1,2)$ and the area bounded by this curve, line $x=4$ and the X -axis is 8 sq . units, then the value of $a-b$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Since the given curve passes through }(1,2) \text {. }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad 2=a+b \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ \text { Area }=\int_0^4 y d x \\ \Rightarrow 8=\int_0^4(a \sqrt{x}+b x) d x \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 8=\left[\mathrm{a} \frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}+\mathrm{b} \frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^4 \\ & \Rightarrow 8=\frac{2 \mathrm{a}}{3} \cdot 8+8 \mathrm{~b} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{a}+3 \mathrm{~b}=3 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

On solving (i) and (ii), we get $\mathrm{a}=3$ and $\mathrm{b}=-1$

$$ \Rightarrow a-b=4 $$

content: The area bounded by the curve $x=2-y-y^2$ and the Y -axis is
explanation:
Given equation of curve

$$ \begin{aligned} & x=2-y-y^2 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \text { Y-axis } \Rightarrow x=0 \end{aligned} $$

Put $x=0$, in equation (i), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 0=2-y-y^2 \\ & \Rightarrow y^2+y-2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(y+2)(y-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y+2=0 \text { or } y=1 \\ & \Rightarrow y=-2 \text { or } y=1 \\ & \text { Required area }=\int_{y=a}^b \mathrm{f}(y) \mathrm{d} y \\ & =\int_{-2}^1\left(2-y-y^2\right) \mathrm{d} y \\ & =\left[2 y-\frac{y^2}{2}-\frac{y^3}{3}\right]_{-2}^1 \\ & =\left(2-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}\right)-\left(-4-\frac{4}{2}+\frac{8}{3}\right) \\ & =\left(\frac{12-3-2}{6}\right)-\left(\frac{-24-12+16}{6}\right) \\ & =\frac{7}{6}-\frac{(-20)}{6} \\ & =\frac{27}{6}=\frac{9}{2} \text { sq. units. } \end{aligned} $$

content: The area of the region bounded by the curve $y=|x-2|$ between $x=1, x=3$ and X -axis is ……
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_1^3|x-2| \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_1^2(2-x) \mathrm{d} x+\int_2^3(x-2) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[2 x-\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_1^2+\left[\frac{x^2}{2}-2 x\right]_2^3 \\ & =\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \\ & =1 \text { sq. unit } \end{aligned} $$

content: The area inside the parabola $y^2=4 \mathrm{a} x$, between the lines $x=\mathrm{a}$ and $x=4 \mathrm{a}$ is equal to
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =2 \int_a^{4 a} \sqrt{4 a x} d x \\ & =4 \sqrt{a} \int_a^{4 a} \sqrt{x} d x \\ & =4 \sqrt{a}\left[\frac{2}{3} x \sqrt{x}\right]_a^{4 a} \\ & =\frac{8}{3} \sqrt{a}[4 a \sqrt{4 a}-a \sqrt{a}] \\ & =\frac{8}{3} \sqrt{a}[8 a \sqrt{a}-a \sqrt{a}] \\ & =\frac{8}{3} \sqrt{a}(7 a \sqrt{a}) \\ & =\frac{56}{3} a^2 \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: The area of the region bounded by $\frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1$ and the line $\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{2}=1$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Required area }=\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{OACB})-\mathrm{A}(\triangle \mathrm{OAB})\\ &\text { Given equation of ellipse is } \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{y^2}{4}=1-\frac{x^2}{9} \\ \therefore & y=\frac{2}{3} \sqrt{9-x^2} \\ & \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{OACB})=\int_0^3 \frac{2}{3} \sqrt{9-x^2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{1}{2} x \sqrt{9-x^2}+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{3}\right]_0^3 \\ & =\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{1}{2} \cdot 3 \sqrt{9-3^2}+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{3}{3}-\frac{1}{2} 0 \sqrt{9-0^2}-\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1} 0\right] \\ & =\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{9}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{3 \pi}{2} \\ & \mathrm{~A}(\Delta \mathrm{OAB})=\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 2=3 \\ \therefore & \text { Required area }=\frac{3 \pi}{2}-3=\frac{3}{2}(\pi-2) \text { sq. units } \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

Alternate method:

The area between $\frac{x^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}+\frac{y^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=1$ and straight line $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$ is $\frac{1}{4} \pi a b-\frac{1}{2} a b$ sq. units.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Required area } & =\frac{1}{4} \pi(3)(2)-\frac{1}{2}(3)(2) \\ & =\frac{3}{2}(\pi-2) \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: The area enclosed between the curves $y^2=4 x$ and $y=|x|$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_0^4 \sqrt{4 x} \mathrm{~d} x-\int_0^4|x| d x \\ & =2 \int_0^4 \sqrt{x} \mathrm{~d} x-\int_0^4 x d x \\ & =2\left[\frac{2}{3} x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right]_0^4-\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^4 \\ & =\frac{4}{3}\left(4^{\frac{3}{2}}-0\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(4^2-0\right) \\ & =\frac{4}{3}(8)-8 \\ & =8\left(\frac{4}{3}-1\right)=\frac{8}{3} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} $$

content: The ratio of the areas bounded by the curves $y=\cos x$ and $y=\cos 2 x$ between $x=0, x=\frac{\pi}{3}$ and X -axis is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}_1=\int_0^{\pi / 3} \cos x \mathrm{~d} x=[\sin x]_0^{\frac{\pi}{3}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \mathrm{~A}_2=\int_0^{\pi / 3} \cos 2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{[\sin x]_0^{\frac{\pi}{3}}}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{~A}_1: \mathrm{A}_2=2: 1 \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in sq. units) bounded between the parabolas $x^2=\frac{y}{4}$ and $x^2=9 y$ and the line $y=2$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Required area }\\ &\begin{aligned} & =2 \int_0^2\left(3 \sqrt{y}-\frac{\sqrt{y}}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} y \\ & =2\left[3\left[\frac{y^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right]_0^2-\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{y^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right]_0^2\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =2\left[2\left(2^{\frac{3}{2}}-0\right)-\frac{1}{3}\left(2^{\frac{3}{2}}-0\right)\right] \\ & =2\left[2(2 \sqrt{2})-\frac{1}{3}(2 \sqrt{2})\right] \\ & =\frac{20 \sqrt{2}}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in sq. units) of the region described by $\left\{(x, y) / y^2 \leq 2 x\right.$ and $\left.y \geq(4 x-1)\right\}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Putting } x=\frac{y^2}{2} \text { in } y=4 x-1 \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & y=4\left(\frac{y^2}{2}\right)-1 \Rightarrow 2 y^2-y-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(y-1)(2 y+1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y=1, \frac{-1}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \text { Required area } \\ & =\int_{-1 / 2}^1\left(\frac{y+1}{4}\right) \mathrm{d} y-\int_{-1 / 2}^1 \frac{y^2}{2} \mathrm{~d} y \\ & =\frac{1}{4}\left[\frac{y^2}{2}+y\right]_{-1 / 2}^1-\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{y^3}{3}\right]_{-1 / 2}^1 \\ & =\frac{1}{4}\left[\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{8}\right)+\left(1+\frac{1}{2}\right)\right]-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{24}\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{15}{8}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{9}{24}\right) \\ & =\frac{15}{32}-\frac{3}{16} \\ & =\frac{9}{32} \end{aligned}$$

content: The area enclosed between the parabola $y^2=4 x$ and the line $y=2 x-4$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Putting } x=\frac{y^2}{4} \text { in } y=2 x-4 \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & y=2\left(\frac{y^2}{4}\right)-4 \\ & \Rightarrow y^2-2 y-8=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(y-4)(y+2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y=4,-2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \text { Required area }=\int_\limits{-2}^4\left(\frac{y+4}{2}-\frac{y^2}{4}\right) \mathrm{d} y \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{y^2}{2}+4 y\right]_{-2}^4-\frac{1}{4}\left[\frac{y^3}{3}\right]_{-2}^4 \\ & =\frac{1}{2}[8+16-(2-8)]-\frac{1}{12}[64-(-8)] \\ & =15-6 \\ & =9 \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the region bounded by curves $y=3 x+1, y=4 x+1$ and $x=2$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_\limits0^2[4 x+1-(3 x+1)] d x \\ & =\int_\limits0^2 x d x=\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^2=2 \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in sq. units) of the region bounded by $y-x=2$ and $x^2=y$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} & y-x=2 \text { and } x^2=y \\ \therefore & x^2-x-2=0 \\ \therefore & (x-2)(x+1)=0 \\ \therefore & x=2 \text { or } x=-1 \\ \therefore & y=4, y=1 \end{array}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Points of intersection of the two curves are $(2,4)$ and $(-1,1)$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_\limits{-1}^2(2+x)-x^2 \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =2[x]_{-1}^2+\frac{1}{2}\left[x^2\right]_{-1}^2-\frac{1}{3}\left[x^3\right]_{-1}^2 \\ & =6+\frac{3}{2}-3=\frac{9}{2} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the region lying in the first quadrant by $y=4 x^2, x=0, y=2, y=4$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_2^4 \frac{\sqrt{y}}{2} \mathrm{~d} y \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left[y^{\frac{3}{2}}\right]_2^4}{\frac{3}{2}} \\ & =\frac{1}{3}(8-2 \sqrt{2}) \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in sq. units) of the region $\left\{(x, y) / x \geq 0, x+y \leq 3, x^2 \leq 4 y\right.$ and $\left.y \leq 1+\sqrt{x}\right\}$ is
explanation:
Given inequalities are

$$\begin{aligned} & x \geq 0 \\ & x+y \leq 3 \\ & x^2 \leq 4 y \\ & y \leq 1+\sqrt{x} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore\quad$ The equalities are

$$\begin{aligned} & x+y=3 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & x^2=4 y \quad\text{.... (ii)}\\ & y=1+\sqrt{x} \quad\text{.... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

from (i) and (iii), we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & 3-x=1+\sqrt{x} \\ \therefore \quad & x+\sqrt{x}-2=0 \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & (\sqrt{x}+2)(\sqrt{x}-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{x}=1 \quad \ldots[\because \sqrt{x} \text { cannot be negative }]\\ & \Rightarrow x=1 \text { and } y=2 \end{aligned}$$

From (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} & x+\frac{x^2}{4}=3 \\ \therefore \quad & x^2+4 x-12=0 \\ \therefore \quad & (x+6)(x-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=2 \quad \ldots[\because x \geq 0]\\ & \Rightarrow y=1 \end{array}\\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \text { Required area }\\ &\begin{aligned} & =\int_0^1\left(1+\sqrt{x}-\frac{x^2}{4}\right) \mathrm{d} x+\int_1^2\left(3-x-\frac{x^2}{4}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_0^1(1+\sqrt{x}) \mathrm{d} x+\int_1^2(3-x)-\frac{1}{4} \int_0^2 x^2 \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =[x]_0^1+\frac{2}{3}\left[x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right]_0^1+3[x]_1^2-\frac{1}{2}\left[x^2\right]_1^2-\frac{1}{12}\left[x^3\right]_0^2 \\ & =1+\frac{2}{3}+3-\frac{3}{2}-\frac{2}{3} \\ & =\frac{5}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Area (in sq.units) lying in the first quadrant and bounded by the circle $x^2+y^2=4$ and the lines $x=0$ and $x=2$ is
explanation:
$\begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2=4 \\ & \Rightarrow y^2=4-x^2 \end{aligned}$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Required area }\\ &\begin{aligned} & =\int_\limits0^2 \sqrt{4-x^2} d x \\ & =\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{4-x^2}+\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]_0^2 \\ & =\left[\frac{2}{2} \sqrt{4-(2)^2}+\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{2}\right)-\frac{0}{2} \sqrt{4-0}-\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{0}{2}\right)\right] \\ & =2 \times \frac{\pi}{2} \\ & =\pi \text { sq. units } \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in sq. units), in the first quadrant bounded by the curve $y=x^2+2$ and the lines $y=x+1, x=0$ and $x=2$, is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_\limits0^2\left[\left(x^2+2\right)-(x+1)\right] \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_\limits0^2\left(x^2-x+1\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\frac{x^3}{3}-\frac{x^2}{2}+x\right]_0^2 \\ & =\frac{8}{3}-2+2-0 \\ & =\frac{8}{3} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in sq. units) bounded by the curves $y=(x+1)^2, y=(x-1)^2$ and the line $y=\frac{1}{4}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =2 \int_0^{\frac{1}{2}}\left[(x-1)^2-\frac{1}{4}\right] \mathrm{d} x \\ & =2\left[\frac{(x-1)^3}{3}\right]_0^{\frac{1}{2}}-\frac{1}{2}[x]_0^{\frac{1}{2}} \\ & =\frac{2}{3}\left(-\frac{1}{8}+1\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}-0\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{3} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in square units) in the first quadrant bounded by the curve $y=x^2+2$ and the lines $y=x+1, x=0$ and $x=3$, is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_\limits0^3\left(x^2+2\right)-(x+1) d x \\ & =\int_\limits0^3\left(x^2-x+1\right) d x \\ & =\left[\frac{x^3}{3}-\frac{x^2}{2}+x\right]_0^3=\frac{15}{2} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area bounded between the curves $y=a x^2$ and $x=a y^2(a>0)$ is 1 sq. units, then the value of a is
explanation:
The two curves intersect at $\mathrm{O}(0,0)$ and $\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}}, \frac{1}{\mathrm{a}}\right)$.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { According to the given condition, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \int_0^{\frac{1}{a}}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{\mathrm{a}}}-\mathrm{a} x^2\right) \mathrm{d} x=1 \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\frac{2}{3 \sqrt{\mathrm{a}}} x^{3 / 2}-\frac{2 x^3}{3}\right]_0^{1 / 2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2}{3 \sqrt{\mathrm{a}}} \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{a}^{3 / 2}}-\frac{\mathrm{a}}{3} \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{a}^3}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2}{3 \mathrm{a}^2}-\frac{1}{3 \mathrm{a}^2}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3 \mathrm{a}^2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \quad \ldots[\because \mathrm{a}>0] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the region, bounded by the parabola $y=x^2+2$ and the lines $y=x, x=0$ and $x=3$, is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_\limits0^3\left(x^2+2-x\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\frac{x^3}{3}+2 x-\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^3 \\ & =9+6-\frac{9}{2}-0 \\ & =\frac{21}{2} \text { sq. unit } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in sq. units) of the region bounded by the curve $x^2=4 y$ and the straight line $x=4 y-2$ is
explanation:
The points of intersection of $x^2=4 y$ and $x=4 y-2$ are $(2,1)$ and $\left(-1, \frac{1}{4}\right)$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Required area } \\ & =\int_{-1}^2 \frac{1}{4}(x+2) \mathrm{d} x-\int_{-1}^2 \frac{1}{4} x^2 \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\frac{1}{4}\left[\frac{x^2}{2}+2 x\right]_{-1}^2-\frac{1}{4}\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-1}^2 \\ & =\frac{9}{8} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in sq. units) bounded by the curves $y=\sqrt{x}, 2 y-x+3=0, X$-axis and lying in the first quadrant is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_\limits0^9 \sqrt{x} \mathrm{~d} x-\int_\limits3^9\left(\frac{x-3}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\frac{2 x^{3 / 2}}{3}\right]_0^9-\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{x^2}{2}-3 x\right]_3^9 \\ & =\frac{2}{3}(27-0)-\frac{1}{2}(36-18) \\ & =9 \text { sq.units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the region bounded by hyperbola $x^2-y^2=9$ and its latus rectum is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & x^2-y^2=9 \\ & \Rightarrow a=b=1 \end{aligned}\\ &\Rightarrow \text { co-ordinates of latus rectum are }\\ &\left( \pm a e, \frac{b^2}{a}\right)=( \pm 3 \sqrt{2}, 3) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \text { Area of hyperbola and its latus rectum } \\ & =4 \int_\limits3^{3 \sqrt{2}} y \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =4 \int_\limits3^{3 \sqrt{2}}\left(\sqrt{x^2-9}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =4\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{x^2-9}-\frac{9}{2} \log \left|x+\sqrt{x^2-9}\right|\right]_3^{3 \sqrt{2}} \\ & =4\left[\left(\frac{3 \sqrt{2}}{2} \sqrt{(3 \sqrt{2})^2-9}-\frac{9}{2} \log \left|3 \sqrt{2}+\sqrt{(3 \sqrt{2})^2-9}\right|\right)\right. \\ & =4\left[\left(\frac{3 \sqrt{2}}{2} \times 3-\frac{9}{2} \log |3 \sqrt{2}+3|+\frac{9}{2} \log 3\right)\right] \\ & =4\left[\frac{9 \sqrt{2}-9}{2}-\frac{9}{2} \log (3 \sqrt{2}+3)+\frac{9}{2} \log 3\right] \\ & =4\left[\frac{\left.\left.9 \sqrt{3^2-9}\right)\right]}{2}-\frac{9}{2} \log \left(\frac{3 \sqrt{2}+3}{3}\right)\right] \\ & =4\left[\frac{9 \sqrt{2}}{2}-\frac{9}{2} \log (\sqrt{2}+1)\right] \\ & =4 \times \frac{9}{2}[\sqrt{2}-\log (\sqrt{2}+1)] \text { sq. units } \\ &=18[\sqrt{2}-\log (\sqrt{2}+1)] \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area bounded by the curve $$y=|x-2|, x=1, x=3$$ and $$X$$-axis is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_\limits1^3|x-2| \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_\limits1^2(2-x) \mathrm{d} x+\int_\limits2^3(x-2) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[2 x-\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_1^2+\left[\frac{x^2}{2}-2 x\right]_2^3 \\ & =\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=1 \text { sq. unit } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area bounded by the curves $$y=(x-1)^2, y=(x+1)^2$$ and $$y=\frac{1}{4}$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =2 \int_\limits0^{\frac{1}{2}}\left[(x-1)^2-\frac{1}{4}\right] \mathrm{d} x \\\\ & =2\left[\frac{(x-1)^3}{3}\right]_0^{\frac{1}{2}}-\frac{1}{2}[x]_0^{\frac{1}{2}} \\\\ & =\frac{2}{3}\left(-\frac{1}{8}+1\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}-0\right) \\\\ & =\frac{1}{3} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: If a curve $$y=a \sqrt{x}+b x$$ passes through the point $$(1,2)$$ and the area bounded by the curve, line $$x=4$$ and $$X$$-axis is 8 sq units, then
explanation:
Given curve $$y=a \sqrt{x}+b x$$ passes through $$(1,2)$$.

The area formed by curve line $$x=4$$ and $$X$$-axis is 8 sq units.

$$\because$$ Since, the curves $$y=a \sqrt{x}+b x$$ passes through $$(1,2)$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad 2 & =a \sqrt{1}+b \cdot 1 \\ & a+b=2 \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Now, the area enclosed by the curve line $$x=4$$ and $$X$$-axis

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int_\limits0^4 y d x=\int_\limits0^4(a \sqrt{x}+b x) d x \\ & =\left[a \cdot \frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}+b \cdot \frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^4 \\ & =\frac{2 a}{3} \cdot 4^{\frac{3}{2}}+\frac{b}{2} \cdot 4^2=\frac{16 a}{3}+8 b \end{aligned}$$

From the problem

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{16 a}{3}+8 b =8 \\ \therefore \quad & 2 a+3 b =3 \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

a = 3, b = $$-$$1

content: The area (in sq. units) of the region bounded by curves $$y=3 x+1, y=4 x+1$$ and $$x=3$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_\limits0^3[4 x+1-(3 x+1)] \mathrm{d} x \\\\ & =\int_\limits0^3 x \mathrm{~d} x \\\\ & =\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^3 \\\\ & =\frac{9}{2} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in sq. units) of the smaller part of the circle $$x^2+y^2=\mathrm{a}^2$$ cut off by the line $$x=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\sqrt{2}}$$ is
explanation:
Substitute $$x=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\sqrt{2}}$$ in $$x^2+y^2=\mathrm{a}^2$$, we get $$\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{2}+y^2=\mathrm{a}^2 \Rightarrow y= \pm \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Required area

$$\begin{aligned} & = 2\int\limits_{{a \over {\sqrt 2 }}}^a {\sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} dx}\\ & =2\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{a^2-x^2}+\frac{a^2}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)\right]_{\frac{a}{\sqrt2}}^2 \\ & =2\left\{\left[0+\frac{a^2}{2} \times \frac{\pi}{2}\right]-\left[\frac{a}{2 \sqrt{2}} \sqrt{a^2-\frac{a^2}{2}}+\frac{a^2}{2} \times \frac{\pi}{4}\right]\right\} \\ & =2\left[\frac{a^2 \pi}{4}-\frac{a^2}{4}-\frac{a^2 \pi}{8}\right] \\ & =\frac{a^2}{2}\left|\pi-1-\frac{\pi}{2}\right| \\ & =\frac{a^2}{2}\left|\frac{\pi}{2}-1\right| \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the region bounded by the curves $$y=\mathrm{e}^x, y=\log x$$ and lines $$x=1, x=2$$ is
explanation:
Required Area

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int_\limits1^2\left(\mathrm{e}^x-\log x\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\mathrm{e}^x\right]_1^2-\int_\limits1^2 1 \log x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\left(\mathrm{e}^2-\mathrm{e}\right)-\left[x \log x-\int_\limits1^2 1 \mathrm{~d} x\right] \\ & =\left(\mathrm{e}^2-\mathrm{e}\right)-[x \log x-x]_1^2 \\ & =\left(\mathrm{e}^2-\mathrm{e}\right)-[(2 \log 2-2)-(1 \log 1-1)] \\ & =\mathrm{e}^2-\mathrm{e}-(2 \log 2-2-0+1) \\ & =\mathrm{e}^2-\mathrm{e}-(2 \log 2-1) \\ & =\left(\mathrm{e}^2-\mathrm{e}+1-2 \log 2\right) \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The slope of the tangent to a curve $$y=\mathrm{f}(x)$$ at $$(x, \mathrm{f}(x))$$ is $$2 x+1$$. If the curve passes through the point $$(1,2)$$, then the area (in sq. units), bounded by the curve, the $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis and the line $$x=1$$, is
explanation:
According to the given condition, $$\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=2 x+1$$

Integrating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get $$y=x^2+x+c$$

As curve passes through $$(1,2)$$, we get $$2=(1)^2+1+c \Rightarrow c=0$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The equation of the curve is $$y=x^2+x$$.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Required area

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int_\limits0^1\left(x^2+x\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\frac{x^3}{3}+\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^1 \\ & =\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{2} \\ & =\frac{5}{6} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the region bounded by the parabola $$y=x^2$$ and the curve $$y=|x|$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =2 \int_\limits0^1\left(x-x^2\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =2\left[\frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^1 \\ & =2\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}\right)=\frac{1}{3} \text { sq.units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area bounded by the $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis and the curve $$y=x(x-2)(x+1)$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text {For X-axis, } \\\\ & y=0 \\\\ & \therefore \quad x(x-2)(x+1)=0 \\\\ & \Rightarrow x=0 \text { or } x=2 \text { or } x=-1 \end{aligned}$$

Required area $$= \int\limits_{ - 1}^0 {y\,dx + \left| {\int\limits_0^2 {y\,dx} } \right|}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int_\limits{-1}^0 x(x-2)(x+1) \mathrm{d} x+\left|\int_\limits0^2 x(x-2)(x+1) \mathrm{d} x\right| \\\\ & =\int_\limits{-1}^0\left(x^3-x^2-2 x\right) \mathrm{d} x+\left|\int_\limits0^2\left(x^3-x^2-2 x\right) \mathrm{d} x\right| \\\\ & =\left[\frac{x^4}{4}-\frac{x^3}{3}-x^2\right]_{-1}^0+\left|\left[\frac{x^4}{4}-\frac{x^3}{3}-x^2\right]_0^2\right| \\\\ & =\frac{5}{12}+\left|-\frac{8}{3}\right| \\\\ & =\frac{37}{12} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: Area of the region bounded by the curve $$y=\sqrt{49-x^2}$$ and $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =2 \int_0^7 \sqrt{49-x^2} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =2\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{49-x^2}+\frac{49}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{7}\right)\right]_0^7 \\ & =2\left[\frac{7}{2} \sqrt{49-49}+\frac{49}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{7}{7}\right)\right]-0 \\ & =2 \times \frac{49}{2} \times \frac{\pi}{2} \\ & =\frac{49 \pi}{2} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in sq. units) bounded by the curve $$y=x|x|, \mathrm{X}$$-axis and the lines $$x=-1$$ and $$x=1$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & y=x|x| \quad \ldots[\text { Given }]\\ & \text { Required area } \\ & =\int_\limits{-1}^1 x|x| \mathrm{d} x \\ & =2 \int_\limits0^1 x^2 \mathrm{~d} x \quad \text{...}[\because \text{Area is always positive]}\\ & =2 \times\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^1 \\ & =2 \times\left(\frac{1}{3}-0\right)=\frac{2}{3} \text { sq.units} \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in sq. units) of the region $$\mathrm{A}=\left\{(x, y) / \frac{y^2}{2} \leq x \leq y+4\right\}$$ is
explanation:
Given that $$\frac{y^2}{2} \leq x \leq y+4$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & x=\frac{y^2}{2} \text { and } x=y+4 \\ & \frac{y^2}{2}=y+4^2 \\ \therefore \quad & y^2-2 y-8=0 \\ \therefore \quad & y=4 \text { or }-2 \\ & \Rightarrow x=8 \text { or } 2 \end{array}$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{A}=\int_\limits{-2}^4\left(y+4-\frac{y^2}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} y$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mathrm{A}=\left[\frac{y^2}{2}+4 y-\frac{y^3}{6}\right]_{-2}^4 \\ & \therefore \quad A=\left(8+16-\frac{64}{6}\right)-\left(2-8+\frac{8}{6}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad A=18 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: The area (in sq. units) of the region described by $$A=\left\{(x, y) / x^2+y^2 \leq 1\right.$$ and $$\left.y^2 \leq 1-x\right\}$$ is
The area of the region bounded by the curve $$y=2 x-x^2$$ and X-axis is
explanation:
Point of intersection of curve $$y=2 x-x^2$$ and $$x$$ axis, is $$0=2 x-x^2 \Rightarrow x(x-2)=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x=0,2$ $

When $$x=0, y=0$$ and when $$x=2, y=0$$.

Refer figure

Required area is shaded.

$$\begin{aligned} A & =\int_\limits0^2\left(2 x-x^2\right) d x \\ & =2\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^2-\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^2 \\ & =(4)-\left(\frac{8}{3}\right)=\frac{4}{3} \text { sq units. } \end{aligned}$$

content: Area bounded by the lines $$y=x, x=-1, x=2$$ and the $$X$$-axis is
explanation:
Required area is sahded.

$$A=\int_\limits{-1}^0 x d x+\int_\limits0^2 x d x$$

$$=\left|\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_{-1}^0\right|+\left|\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^2\right|=\frac{1}{2}+2=\frac{5}{2}$$

content: The area of the region included between the parabolas $$y^2=8 x$$ and $$x^2=8 y$$, is
explanation:
Refer figure

Required area is shaded. Point of intersection of given curves are $$y^2=8 x$$ and $$x^2=8 y$$ i.e.

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{x^2}{8}\right)^2=8 x \Rightarrow x\left(x^3-512\right)=0 \\ & \therefore O \equiv(0,0) \text { and } P \equiv(8,8) \\ & \therefore A=\int_\limits0^8(2 \sqrt{2})(\sqrt{x}) d x-\int_\limits0^8 \frac{x^2}{8} d x \end{aligned}$$

$$=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)}\left[x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right]_0^8-\frac{1}{24}\left[x^3\right]_0^8=\left(\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3}\right)(8 \sqrt{8})-\frac{1}{24}(512)=\frac{64}{3} \text { sq. units }$$

content: The area bounded by the parabola $$y^2=4 a x$$ and its latus-rectum $$x=a$$ is
explanation:
Refer figure.

Required area is shaded.

Point of intersection of $$x=a$$ and $$y^2=4 a x$$, is $$y^2=4 a^2 \quad \Rightarrow y= \pm 2 a$$ and $$x=a \Rightarrow(a, \pm 2 a)$$

$$\therefore A=2 \int_\limits\limits0^a(2 \sqrt{a} \sqrt{x}) d x$$

$$=4 \sqrt{a} \int_\limits0^a x^{\frac{1}{2}} d x=4 \sqrt{a}\left[\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)}\right]_0^a=(4 \sqrt{a})\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)(a \sqrt{a})=\frac{8}{3} a^2 \text { sq. units }$$

content: The area bounded by the parabola $$y^2=x$$ and the line $$x+y=2$$ in the first quadrant is
explanation:
The point of intersection of $$y^2=x$$ and $$x+y=2$$ is,

$$(2-x)^2=x \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^2-5 x+4=0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad(x-4)(x-1)=0$$

Let $$A=(1,1)$$ in first quadrant and $$B=(4,-2)$$ in fourth quadrant

The line $$x+y=2$$ cuts $$X$$ axis at $$P(2,0)$$

Refer figure

Required area is shaded.

$$\therefore A=\int_0^1 \sqrt{x} d x+\int_1^2(2-x) d x$$

$$=\left[\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)}\right]_0^1+[2 x]_1^2-\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_1^2$$

$$=\left[\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)(1)\right]+[2(2-1)]-\left[\left(\frac{4-1}{2}\right)\right]=\frac{2}{3}+2-\frac{3}{2}=\frac{7}{6}$$ sq. units

content: The area bounded between the curve $$x^2=y$$ and the line $$y=4 x$$ is
explanation:
Refer figure,

Required area is shaded.

Point of intersection of given curves are (0, 0) and (4, 16)

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore A & =\int_\limits0^4\left(4 x-x^2\right) d x \\ & =4 \int_\limits0^4 x d x-\int_\limits0^4 x^2 d x=4\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^4-\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^4 \\ & =2(16)-\frac{(4)^3}{3}=32-\frac{64}{3}=\frac{32}{3} \text { sq. units }\end{aligned}$$

content: The area bounded by the parabola $$y^2=x$$, the straight line $$y=4$$ and $$Y$$ axis is
explanation:
Refer figure

Required area is shaded.

Point of intersection of $$y^2=x$$ and $$y=4$$ is $$A \equiv(16,4)$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore A & =\int_\limits0^4 y^2 d y \\ & =\left[\frac{y^3}{3}\right]_0^4=\frac{64}{3} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the region bounded by the curve y$$^2$$ = 4x and the line y = x is
explanation:
Refer figure. Point of intersection of given curves $$x^2=4x \Rightarrow x(x-4)=0$$

$$\therefore 0 \equiv(0,0) \text { and } P \equiv(4,4) $$

Required area is shaded.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore A & =\int_\limits0^4(\sqrt{4 x}-x) d x=2 \int_\limits0^4 x^{\frac{1}{2}} d x-\int_\limits\theta^4 x d x \\ & =2\left[\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)}\right]_0^4-\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^4=\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)(4 \times 2)-\frac{16}{2} \\ & =\frac{32}{3}-\frac{16}{2}=\frac{16}{6}=\frac{8}{3} \text { sq. units. } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area bounded by the parabola $$y=x^2$$ and the line $$y=x$$ is
explanation:
The required area is shaded.

The point of intersection of the curves are

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{x}^2=\mathrm{x} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)=0 \text { i.e. } \mathrm{O}(0,0) \text { and } \mathrm{P}(1,1) \\ \therefore \mathrm{A} & =\int_\limits0^1\left(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^2\right) \mathrm{dx} \\ & =\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{2}\right]_0^1-\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}^3}{3}\right]_0^1=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{1}{6} \text { sq. units }\end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the region bounded by the parabola x$$^2$$ = y and the line y = x is
explanation:
Points of intersection of given curves are

$$\mathrm{x^2-x=0\Rightarrow x(x-1)=0\Rightarrow x=0,1}$$

i.e. O(0, 0) and A(1, 1)

Required area is shaded.

$$\therefore$$ $$A = \int\limits_0^1 {(x - {x^2})dx} $$

$$ = \left[ {{{{x^2}} \over 2}} \right]_0^1 - \left[ {{{{x^3}} \over 3}} \right]_0^1 = {1 \over 2} - {1 \over 3} = {1 \over 6}$$ sq. units

content: The area of the region bounded by the curve $y=\sin x$ between $x=-\pi$ and $x=\frac{3 \pi}{2}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given curve, }\\ &\begin{gathered} y=\sin x \\ x=-\pi=\frac{3 \pi}{2} \end{gathered} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Area of shaded region, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \left|\int_\limits{-\pi}^0 \sin x d x\right|+\int_\limits0^\pi \sin x d x+\left|\int_\limits\pi^{\pi / 2} \sin x d x\right| \\ & \left|-[\cos x]_{-\pi}^0\right|+[-\cos x]_0^\pi+\left|-[\cos x]_\pi^{\frac{3 \pi}{2}}\right| \\ & \quad=|-1-1|+(1+1)+|0-1| \\ & \quad=2+2+1=5 \text { sq unit } \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The area included between the parabolas $$y^2=5 x$$ and $$x^2=5 y$$ is
explanation:
We know that, area included between $$x^2=a y$$ and $$y^2=a x$$ is $$\frac{a^2}{3}$$ sq units

$$\therefore$$ Area included between $$x^2=5 y$$ and $$y^2=5 x$$ is $$\frac{(5)^2}{3} =\frac{25}{3}$$ sq units.

content: The area of the region bounded by the curve $$y=4 x^3-6 x^2+4 x+1$$ and the lines $$x=1, x=5$$ and $$X$$-axis is
explanation:
Area of region bounded by the curve $$y=4 x^3-6 x^2+4 x+1$$ and line $$x=1, x=5$$ and $$X$$ - axis, is

$$\begin{aligned} & \int_1^5\left(4 x^3-6 x^2+4 x+1\right) d x \\ & =\left[x^4-2 x^3+2 x^2+x\right]_1^5 \\ & =[(625-250+50+5)-(1-2+2+1)] \\ & =430-2=428 \text { sq unit } \end{aligned}$$

content: . Area of the region bounded by $y=\cos x, x=0$, $x=\pi$ and $X$-axis is ... sq. units.
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =2 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} y d x \\ & =2 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cos x d x \\ & =2(\sin x)_0^{\pi / 2} \\ & =2(1-0) \\ & =2 \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$$

content: The area of the region enclosed between pair of the lines $x y=0$ and the lines $x y+5 x-4 y-20=0$, is .............
explanation:
Given pair of lines

$$\begin{aligned} & x y+5 x-4 y-20=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x(y+5)-4(y+5)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(y+5)(x-4)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=4, y=-5 \\ & \text { and also, } x y=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=0, y=0 \end{aligned}$$

Now, draw the graph of the pairs of line

$\therefore$ Required area of bounded region $=4 \times 5=20$ sq. units

content: The area of the region bounded by the curve $y=2 x-x^2$ and the line $y=x$ is ........... units. square
explanation:
The intersection points of given curves are $(0,0)$ and $(1,1)$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Required area } & =\int_0^1\left(\left(2 x-x^2\right)-x\right] d x \\ & =\int_0^1\left(x-x^2\right) d x=\left[\frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^1 \end{aligned}$$

$=\frac{1}{6}$ sq. unit
